coredata.dc:description: The inherent conflict between self-healing and wear resistance has long been a key issue in materials science. Driven by the goals of economic and social sustainability, we are committed to finding an ideal balance between these two seemingly opposing properties through molecular structure design and reinforcement optimization. On this foundation, we have innovatively integrated damage monitoring into the coating material. Herein, an advanced multifunctional waterborne polyurethane-based composite coating (BNC-SWPU) was constructed for the first time by innovatively combining self-healing, wear resistance and damage monitoring. These properties were conferred by a design strategy that combined modified boron nitride nanosheets/carbon quantum dots (BNON/CQD) hybrid fillers with multiple dynamic reversible bonds (disulfide and hydrogen bonds). BNON/CQD fillers synergistically enhanced the wear resistance of coatings through the interlayer slip of BNON and the nano-bearing-like structure exhibited by CQD at the sliding interface. Meanwhile, the composite coatings possessed excellent self-healing ability under both scratch and wear due to the easy migration of molecular chains and the capability of the dynamic bonds to break and reform in response to thermal stimuli. The composite coatings, due to the introduction of CQD with fluorescent properties, were endowed with an intense blue emission capability under UV light. Upon damage, emission intensity diminished but recovered post-self-healing. Consequently, this innovative design provides new ideas for the development of smart materials, while the multifunctionality it gives to composite coatings shows great potential for practical applications.

head.ce:title: Advanced multifunctional self-healing composites for the integration of wear resistance and damage monitoring

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inherent conflict between self-healing and wear resistance has long been a key issue in materials science. Driven by the goals of economic and social sustainability, we are committed to finding an ideal balance between these two seemingly opposing properties through molecular structure design and reinforcement optimization. On this foundation, we have innovatively integrated damage monitoring into the coating material. Herein, an advanced multifunctional waterborne polyurethane-based composite coating (BNC-SWPU) was constructed for the first time by innovatively combining self-healing, wear resistance and damage monitoring. These properties were conferred by a design strategy that combined modified boron nitride nanosheets/carbon quantum dots (BNON/CQD) hybrid fillers with multiple dynamic reversible bonds (disulfide and hydrogen bonds). BNON/CQD fillers synergistically enhanced the wear resistance of coatings through the interlayer slip of BNON and the nano-bearing-like structure exhibited by CQD at the sliding interface. Meanwhile, the composite coatings possessed excellent self-healing ability under both scratch and wear due to the easy migration of molecular chains and the capability of the dynamic bonds to break and reform in response to thermal stimuli. The composite coatings, due to the introduction of CQD with fluorescent properties, were endowed with an intense blue emission capability under UV light. Upon damage, emission intensity diminished but recovered post-self-healing. Consequently, this innovative design provides new ideas for the development of smart materials, while the multifunctionality it gives to composite coatings shows great potential for practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Composite coating, as a crucial protective layer, plays a vital role in enhancing the surface properties of a substrate or component, including wear resistance and mechanical strength. It can effectively prolong the service life of equipment or components and improve their overall performance. This efficient and convenient protective measure is widely recognized and applied in practice[1,2]. Among many coating materials, polymer resins are widely used in protective coatings due to their excellent adhesion, mechanical properties, superior processability, and molding flexibility. For example, epoxy, phenolic, and polyurethane are the most commonly used basic resins for protective coatings [3–5]. They can not only meet the application requirements in various complex environments, but also provide long-lasting protective effects for the coatings. Waterborne polyurethane (WPU) has been extensively researched as a versatile polymer with excellent comprehensive performance and environmental friendliness. This eco-friendly coating can not only be used for traditional metal protection, equipment coating and furniture painting, but it can also be extended to high-end fields such as automobiles, spacecraft components and precision instruments [6–8]. However, due to friction and wear from daily use, the coating will inevitably suffer damage such as cracks and scratches, which can further lead to more severe damage. This, in turn, increases the frequency of repairs and replacements, ultimately resulting in a waste of resources [9–11]. To address this issue, the development of self-healing and wear-resistant WPU coating materials, with the integration of damage monitoring functions into the coating, holds significant practical importance for extending the service life of the substrate.

Timely self-healing of damaged coatings is crucial for enhancing their durability and maintenance efficiency. In the field of materials science, incorporating reversible covalent or reversible non-covalent bonds into the molecular chains and utilizing their dynamic characteristics to confer self-healing capabilities to materials has become a common and effective method [12–14]. For example, a waterborne polyurethane coating with self-healing properties based on dynamic disulfide bonds was prepared by Ye et al. [15]. However, the inherent contradiction between the wear resistance and self-healing properties of polymer coatings poses a challenge in developing a harmonized and comprehensive coating system[16,17]. At the level of molecular structure design, achieving WPU coatings with wear resistance necessitates robust mechanical properties. This typically involves selecting high molecular weight monomers or introducing monomers with rigid groups for cross-linking to form polymers. Self-healing properties, in turn, require the polymer to be flexible, which requires the introduction of flexible chain structures and dynamic reversible bonds in the design. Considering the inherent conflict between these two properties, the wear resistance and self-healing properties are balanced to a certain extent by adjusting or designing the molecular structure during the preparation of WPU matrix [18,19]. On the one hand, appropriate mechanical properties can be conferred by rationalizing the ratio of soft to hard segments [20]. On the other hand, the introduction of aromatic reversible disulfide bonds and hydrogen bonds between molecular chains with functional groups endows the WPU coating with multiple dynamic self-healing capabilities (SWPU) [21,22]. At the same time, due to the high bonding and dissociation energies of these aromatic disulfide bonds, as well as the interaction forces between the molecular chains, the coating is able to resist damage caused by external friction. Therefore, the introduction of dynamic reversible bonds into the molecular structure of the materials is expected to produce self-healing materials with good wear resistance.

There are some overwhelming problems existed in practical applications. This is reflected in the fact that while some scratches can be directly observed with the naked eye, minor or early wear inside mechanical equipment, in confined spaces, and in specific environments (e.g., dark environments) is often difficult to detect with the naked eye. These defects can be detrimental to the structural integrity of the material over time [23–26]. Therefore, effective wear indicators play a vital role in coating life management and prevention of coating failures. We have developed a simple, low-cost and efficient method for designing the introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQD) with fluorescence effect, specifically for wear monitoring purposes. Considering that fluorescence effect monitoring of wear condition has unique advantages in terms of minute wear monitoring, monitoring of complex environments or hidden parts, real-time monitoring needs, and non-contact monitoring. Therefore, it is an innovative and promising approach to dope CQD with fluorescence effect into wear-resistant coatings. This change in fluorescence can be used as an indication of wear, thus enabling timely monitoring and early warning of coating damage [27,28]. Meanwhile, CQD, by virtue of their nanoscale size, exhibit a unique “nano-bearing” effect during the friction process, forming a rolling layer between the coating and its friction partner, which reduces the friction coefficient and the degree of wear to a certain extent [29–31]. Additionally, to enhance the basic wear resistance of SWPU coatings, an effective method involves preparing functionalized boron nitride (BNON) through chitosan-assisted ball-milling exfoliation and further utilizing isocyanate grafting to improve their interfacial bonding ability with the polymer matrix [32–34]. This approach can significantly enhance the tribological properties of composite coatings.

Herein, we propose a novel tactic to synchronously develop an advanced composite coating that integrates self-healing, wear resistance and fluorescent damage monitoring capabilities. The introduction of 4-Aminophenyl disulfide (APDS) into the molecular chain of WPU and the introduction of modified BNON/CQD fillers into SWPU effectively achieve the perfect synergy between high wear resistance and macro self-healing properties of the composite coatings. Therefore, for the first time, the innovative combination of self-healing, wear resistance and damage monitoring capabilities, simultaneously imparted to SWPU composite coatings, is of far-reaching practical relevance to significantly extend the service life of their substrates.

The h-BN powder with transversal size of 5–10 μm, 4-Aminophenyl disulfide (APDS), 4,6-Diaminopynmidine (DAP), Polypropylene carbonate diol (PPCD, Mn=2000) and Triethylamine (TEA) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA) and Urea (H2NCONH2) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Isophorone Diisocyanate (IPDI) and 2,2-Bis(hydroxymethyl)propionic acid (DMPA) were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd. Chitosan (CS) was supplied by Shanghai Zhongqin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL) was purchased from Shanghai Meryer Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of chitosan-functionalized and isocyanate-grafted BNNSs
ce:section.ce:para: The h-BN powder and Chitosan (CS) were loaded into a Teflon tank at a mass ratio of 1:2 and rotated in a planetary ball mill to obtain BNO powder. Subsequently, BNO powder was dispersed in DMF solution (30 mg/mL) and sonicated for 1 h to form a homogeneous suspension. IPDI and catalyst DBTDL were then added to the suspension. The reaction was carried out with uniform stirring at 80°C for 3 h in a protective nitrogen gas atmosphere. The solution was centrifuged, washed and dried to obtain isocyanate-functionalized BNNSs (BNON).

The synthesis of CQD using a hydrothermal method was based on previous literature [35,36]. Citric acid and urea were dissolved in deionized water at a mass ratio of 2:1. The mixed solution was placed in a PTFE liner in a reaction kettle in an oven at 160°C for 6 h. Finally, CQD powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SWPU emulsion and its composite coating
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, PPCD (1.5 mmol) was dehydrated and added with IPDI (6.8 mmol) and DMPA (1.7 mmol) in a three-neck flask at 80°C for 3 h. And DBTDL was added as catalyst. Afterwards, APDS (0.75 mmol) and DAP (0.64 mmol) were added and reacted with isocyanate for 1 h. After the reaction temperature dropped to 50°C, TEA (1.7 mmol) was introduced and reacted for 30 min. After that, the synthesized mixture was fed quickly to deionized water for high-speed dispersion for 1 h to obtain SWPU emulsion [37].

BNON at different mass fractions (0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 1.5 wt%, 2 wt%, 2.5 wt%) and 0.35 wt% CQD were added into SWPU, then those composite emulsions were magnetically stirred for 2 h and sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was coated on Q235 steel and cured at room temperature for 1 day, most of the water evaporated. The prepared BNON/CQD-SWPU composite coating could be further dried in an oven at 60°C, named BNC1-SWPU, BNC2-SWPU, BNC3-SWPU, BNC4-SWPU and BNC5-SWPU. In addition, SWPU and CQD-SWPU coating were obtained using the same procedure.

The morphologies of filler were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU8600), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai F30), and atomic force microscope (AFM, LabRAM Odyssey+SmartSPM). The chemical component and bonding of samples were proved by X-ray diffraction (XRD, EMPYREAN), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR, NEXUE 670), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS SUPRA+). Thermal analysis of materials was performed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, MCA125S-2CCN-1), thermal conductivity (TC, LFA467HT), and infrared thermography (Fotric323pro). The tensile stress-strain properties of samples were evaluated using a universal materials testing machine (68TM-5). Each sample was prepared as a strip with dimensions of 50 mm × 10 mm × 5 mm. At least three samples were prepared for each testing.

The tribological properties of composite coating were investigated by using a ball-disk friction and wear tester (MS-T3001). The 304-steel ball with a diameter of 6 mm was selected as friction counterpart. The experimental conditions were continuous rotation for 30 min under an applied load of 5 N and a rotation radius of 5 mm. The test was carried out by point contact rotational friction. At least three samples were prepared for each tribological testing. The wear volume (ΔV) and wear rate (Ws), two important parameters of tribological performance, were calculated by the following formulas [38,39]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and characterization of fillers and BNC-SWPU coating
ce:section.ce:para: The introduction of BNON/CQD filler into the WPU matrix is important for the construction of protective polymer coatings on steel surfaces. The preparative and mechanism route of modified BN, CQD filler and its composite fluorescent coating were shown in Fig. 1. To enhance the dispersion and compatibility of BN within polymer composites, the pristine h-BN ball milling modification and grafting modification were illustrated in Fig. 1b-c. Firstly, BN underwent fracture, thinning, and physical adsorption reactions when in close contact with the CS powder, due to the combined shear and impact forces exerted by the balls and interactions with CS molecules, resulting in the formation of CS-functionalized BNNSs (BNO). Subsequently, the reactivity of the hydroxyl functional group was utilized to obtain isocyanate-grafted BNNSs by chemical reaction with IPDI monomers required for the preparation of WPU (BNON). This enabled the inorganic material to become a constituent unit of the composite polymer, which helped to realize the chemical bonding between the inorganic material and organic polymer, thereby improving their compatibility and interfacial bonding [40]. Fluorescent CQD were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as raw materials (Fig. 1d). Due to the presence of a large number of reactive groups, this not only conferred good water solubility to CQD, but also offered the possibility of combining them with other substances (Fig. 1e).

The development of coatings with wear resistant properties and rapid self-healing capabilities remains a formidable challenge. Considering the balance between tribological performance and self-healing efficiency, an effective molecular structure design of the WPU coatings is required. The synthesis process of SWPU and its composites was illustrated in Fig. 2. PPCD as the soft segment and IPDI as the hard segment were the key raw materials constituting the polyurethane backbone. The mechanical properties of WPU, such as hardness and elongation, could be controlled by adjusting the ratio between these two segments. DMPA served as a hydrophilic chain extender, providing carboxylic acid groups. Meanwhile, TEA acted as a neutralizing agent to neutralize carboxylic acid groups in the prepolymer, facilitating water dispersion and emulsion stability. More importantly, dynamic reversible disulfide bonds were introduced into the structure of WPU, endowing WPU with the ability of self-healing. A small amount of DAP was also added to optimize the key properties of SWPU, such as abrasion resistance and self-healing ability [41]. Its main purpose was to utilize nitrogen atoms in the pyridine ring and amino groups within the DAP molecule to form a network of multiple hydrogen bonds with urea bonds, urethane bonds, and water molecules in the polyurethane main chain. The dynamic properties of hydrogen bonding and the rigid structure of the pyridine ring worked in concert to comprehensively optimize the mechanical and self-healing properties of the materials. Thus, these monomers modulated the balance between tribological properties and self-healing ability at the molecular level by in situ polymerization method. Following the addition of lubricating BNON and fluorescent CQD fillers to SWPU emulsion, we utilized their individual effects and synergies to enhance wear resistance and enable a wear monitoring function.

The morphology and dimensions of BN and CQD fillers were shown in Fig. 3. Compared with original h-BN (Fig. 3a), BNON nanosheets, which had been ball-milled with the assistance of the CS crystals and successfully modified by isocyanate grafting, not only had their size and thickness effectively reduced, but also exhibited excellent dispersion characteristics without significant agglomeration (Fig. 3b). Meanwhile, the EDS mapping of the modified BNON showed that the elements B, N, C and O, belonging to BN and modifier (Fig. 3e). The TEM images of BNON were shown in Fig. 3c-d, where few-layer BNON nanosheets could be observed. The measured distance of 0.35 nm was close to the (002) interplanar spacing of h-BN, indicating that most of the crystalline structure of BNON was retained. The AFM was used to measure the lateral dimensions and thickness of exfoliated BNON nanosheets, whose AFM image and the corresponding height profiles were provided in Fig. 3f-g. The statistical analysis showed that the lateral size and thickness were around 50–600 nm and 2–5 nm, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 3h-i, the TEM images revealed that CQD particles exhibited a uniformly dispersed spherical morphology, with a particle size distribution ranging from 1 to 3 nm.

Based on the modification and preparation of BN and CQD fillers, as well as the molecular structure design of SWPU matrix mentioned above, we analyzed the materials using FTIR, XRD, Raman spectra and XPS characterization means. The FTIR spectra of fillers, SWPU matrix and its composite coating were shown in Fig. 4a-b. In CQD (Fig. 4a), the broad stretching vibration absorption bands of O-H and N-H were observed at 3000–3500 cm−1. The presence of these functional groups made CQD well dispersed in water [42]. Two intense peaks of BN at 1323 cm−1 and 762 cm−1 were indexed to B-N stretching mode and bending mode, respectively. After ball milling and chemical modification, obvious hydroxyl and amine absorption peaks were observed in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1. At this time, the stretching and bending peaks of B-N shifted from 1323 cm−1 and 762 cm−1 to 1365 cm−1 and 804 cm−1, respectively. This result indicated the formation of hydrogen bonds and intermolecular binding interactions between CS and BNNSs [43]. Meanwhile, it was found that B-O-C and weak -NCO peaks, which indicated that BN nanosheets were modified to disperse more uniformly and form stronger chemical bonding with SWPU, thereby providing good compatibility for subsequent bonding with SWPU matrix. In addition, the successful modification of BNON was further illustrated by combining XRD in Fig. 4c and the Raman spectra in Fig. 4d. In particular, the characteristic peak position of BNON in the (002) plane remained unchanged after exfoliation, but the peak width increased, and a low and broad diffraction peak appeared around 20°. This indicated that the mechanical ball milling process led to the exfoliation and fracture of BNON, resulting in the formation of smaller grains and possibly amorphous or disordered structures. It was evident that the Raman curve of BNON exhibited a slight right shift and a decrease in intensity, which were attributed to the thinning of the BN thickness and the weakening of the interlayer force, further indicating that both the size and the number of layers of the BN had been reduced after peeling. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 4b), no characteristic peak of the -NCO group was displayed at 2270 cm−1, indicating that there was no unreacted IPDI in the prepared WPU and SWPU. Based on this, we tentatively concluded that the synthesis of WPU was successful. In addition, in contrast to WPU curve, the FTIR spectrum of SWPU showed an S-S absorption peak at 518 cm−1. To further identify the disulfide bond, the Raman spectrum of SWPU coating was shown in Fig. 4e. Compared with WPU, the band at 521 cm−1 was assigned to the disulfide [44,45]. Combined with the conclusion of the FTIR spectrum, indicating that APDS was successfully introduced into WPU. The XPS characterization was used to further understand the construction by analyzing the chemical compositions in the BNC4-SWPU coating (Fig. 4f-i). The obtained BNC4-SWPU coating was mainly composed of O, N, C, B and S elements. Although element B exhibited a relatively low peak intensity in the full spectrum analysis due to its limited addition, by combining the XRD test results, we were able to clearly observe the new diffraction peaks presented by BNC4-SWPU composite coating compared to the pure SWPU. Notably, the (002) crystalline facet, which was a characteristic feature of BNON nanosheets, was observed, further confirming that BNON nanosheets had been successfully and effectively embedded within the SWPU matrix. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s could be deconvoluted into three peaks that were assigned to N-C, N-H and N-B bonds, respectively. The O1s spectrum was divided into two peaks at 532.1 eV (CO) and 532.8 eV (C-O-C/C-OH). The XRD and FTIR results confirmed the composition and type of functional groups. The C-S and S-S peaks shown in Fig. 4i were consistent with the results of FTIR and Raman, indicating that the S-S bond had been successfully introduced into SWPU, thereby endowing the composite coatings with a certain level of self-healing ability [46].

The optical colors of SWPU and BNC4-SWPU composites under visible and UV light were shown in Fig. 5a-f. It could be clearly observed that both emulsion and coating exhibited fluorescence under UV light [47]. Fig. 5 g displayed the surface SEM image of BNC4-SWPU coating, and the elemental distribution depicted in EDS mapping image indicated that fillers were evenly distributed throughout the coating. The synthesized SWPU and BNC4-SWPU emulsions were subjected to particle size determination as a means of evaluating their storage stability. The particle size of SWPU and BNC4-SWPU were found to be around 50.6 nm and 80.2 nm, respectively. Compared with pure SWPU, the particle size distribution curve of BNC4-SWPU became higher and narrower, which implied a more concentrated particle size distribution, and these fillers were able to be uniformly dispersed in SWPU emulsion, thus indicating good compatibility with SWPU matrix. Fig. 5i showed the fluorescence emission spectra of CQD-SWPU coating at different excitation wavelengths. The introduction of CQD filler into SWPU emulsion endowed SWPU coating with excellent photoluminescence properties. It could be found that when the excitation wavelength was less than 380 nm, the photoluminescence intensity gradually increased with the increase of the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength was 380 nm, the photoluminescence intensity reached the maximum value, and then the photoluminescence intensity started to decrease with the increase of the excitation wavelength. In addition, the analogous effect was detected in the fluorescence properties of BNC4-SWPU coating with the introduction of BNON lubricating filler, thereby conclusively affirming the successful integration of nanofiller into SWPU without detrimental effects on tis inherent specific attributes (Fig. 5j). Ultimately, unlike pure SWPU, the coating prepared by incorporating CQD with fluorescent effects was capable of efficiently absorbing excitation light and converting it into fluorescence emission within this specific wavelength range, thus exhibiting excellent photoluminescence performance. Fig. 5k demonstrated the average particle size and Zeta potential of SWPU-based composite emulsions before and after modification with h-BN. It could be seen that the average particle size of BNC4-SWPU composite emulsion was reduced compared to that of the composite emulsion with unmodified filler, indicating that the modified BN was dispersed more uniformly within the SWPU matrix. In addition, the absolute value of Zeta potential of BNC4-SWPU composite emulsion increased compared to that of the unmodified system, which helped prevent particle agglomeration and further improved the stability of the dispersion system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal and mechanical properties of BNC-SWPU coating
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability of SWPU coating and its composite coating were evaluated by thermogravimetric analysis ( Fig. 6a). The TGA curves of the samples showed essentially similar thermal decomposition behavior, which appeared to be predominantly a two-stage thermal decomposition. The first stage (200°C to 300°C) was caused by the structural instability of the hard molecular chain segments or due to the breakdown of carbamate bonds. The second decomposition stage, ranging from 300°C to 400°C, was derived from the chain scission of the soft segment polyol [48]. The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature at which a material transforms from the glassy state to a highly elastic state, reflecting the motility of the molecular chain segments of the material, which is crucial for evaluating the self-healing performance of the coating. As shown in Fig. 6b, the Tg of SWPU and its composite coating was around 24.5°C. Therefore, when the temperature rose to or above this Tg, the motility of the molecular chain segments increased, thereby facilitating the reconnection of molecular chains and the formation of crosslinked networks during the self-healing process [49]. In order to visually and effectively evaluate the thermal properties of the material, we conducted tests on the samples for thermal conductivity and infrared thermography (Fig. 6c-d). The thermal conductivity of our prepared pure SWPU was approximately 0.32 W/(m·K). With the addition of thermally conductive and lubricating fillers, the thermal conductivity of BNC5-SWPU reached its highest value, which was 23 % higher than that of pure SWPU. This enhancement was attributed to the high thermal conductivity of fillers themselves, as well as their ability to form an effective thermal conductivity network within the resin matrix [50]. Furthermore, the infrared thermography recorded the variation in surface temperature over time during the heating process of the materials. The composite material exhibited a faster heat transfer rate compared to pure SWPU, indicating that the fillers within the matrix were effective in transmitting heat.

The mechanical properties of the samples, including tensile strength and elongation at break, were demonstrated in the stress-strain curves in Fig. 6e. Compared with the original SWPU, the mechanical properties of CQD-SWPU composites were significantly enhanced. The CQD-SWPU composites exhibited superior tensile strength, primarily attributed to the rolling mechanism of the nano-like bearing structure of CQD between the molecular chains. This mechanism effectively dispersed stress concentration under external force, thereby improving the tensile strength of the composites. Secondly, the surface of CQD was rich in polar groups, such as hydroxyl and amino groups. These polar functional groups could form strong interfacial interactions with the resin matrix. Such interactions promoted a closer arrangement of molecular chains, thereby enhancing the stress transfer efficiency within the material and further strengthening the mechanical properties of the composites. Meanwhile, the results showed that the mechanical properties of composites were significantly enhanced with increasing dosage of BNON nanosheets, compared to the pristine SWPU [51]. Specifically, the tensile strength of the composite film was 1.4 times higher than that of pure SWPU when BNON nanosheets were added at a content of 2 wt%. This was attributed to the fact that BN nanosheets themselves had high strength and rigidity, which could play a reinforcing role in the SWPU matrix. Additionally, it was due to the chemical reaction between the modified BN nanosheets and SWPU matrix, which could achieve a more uniform dispersion and thus enhance the tensile strength of composites. However, the rigid BN nanosheets restrict the movement of the polyurethane chain segments, and this restriction leads to an increased likelihood of polymer chain breakage during stretching, resulting in a decrease in the elongation at break of composite. These results indicated that BNON nanosheets could improve the mechanical properties of SWPU to a certain extent. Water contact angles measurement provided further insight into the surface properties of SWPU and its composite coatings (Fig. 6f). The water contact angles of composite coatings exhibited a slightly upward trending with the addition of filler content. This phenomenon was due to the fact that BN filler itself had a low surface energy, and when its content in the coating increased, the overall surface free energy of coatings might decrease, and the surface of coatings might become rougher and form more micro-nano structures, which affected the magnitude of water contact angle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-healing performance, scratch wear detection and self-healing monitoring function of coatings
ce:section.ce:para: In practical applications, protective coatings with excellent mechanical properties inevitably suffer additional damage, including nicks, cuts and scratches. Therefore, self-healing capability is critical to the durability of coatings. In this study, excellent self-healing capabilities of SWPU coatings are attributed to the combined effect of multiple dynamic reversible bonds consisting of disulfide bonds and intermolecular hydrogen bonds. During the preparation process, we deliberately introduced substances containing dynamically reversible disulfide bonds to form these disulfide bonds. At the same time, the formation of a hydrogen bonding network played a key role. This network consisted of urethane and urea bonds on the polyurethane molecule chain, as well as hydrogen bonds formed between the nitrogen atoms of the pyridine rings in the introduced DAP molecules and the urethane bonds on the polyurethane chains. To qualitatively analyze the self-healing effect of SWPU and its composites, we used a blade to make an incision in the coating and observed the healing of incision under an optical microscope at different temperatures and times. The specimens were cut with deeper incisions as shown in Fig. 7. The self-healing process of SWPU and BNC4-SWPU coatings was observed at temperatures of 30, 50, 70, 90 and 110°C (Fig. 7a-f). The results showed that the incisions on all coatings exhibited significant signs of healing, which was mainly due to the relatively low Tg of SWPU and the introduction of dynamic reversible bonds. Notably, the coated incisions achieved excellent self-healing in a shorter period of time as the temperature increased (Fig. 7 g). The reason for this was that the elevated temperature significantly accelerated the motion of polymer molecules, which in turn facilitated the breaking and re-formation of reversible dynamic bonds, thereby greatly accelerating the healing rate. This also reflected that the prepared coatings had certain thermo-responsive self-healing performance and were able to repair the damage by the movement of polymer molecular chain segments and the re-formation of dynamic reversible bonds at a certain temperature. It could also be observed that SWPU coating healed a little better than BNC4-SWPU coating, which was attributed to the fact that filler particles might act as physical barriers, restricting or hindering the movement of the polymer molecular chain segments. Furthermore, the healing degree increased slowly with prolonged healing time, possibly due to the stronger interaction force between the coating and the steel substrate. This strong interaction provided better adhesion and initial self-healing ability. However, during prolonged repair periods, the coating might be subjected to a large stress concentration at the incision due to the greater hardness and rigidity of the steel, thereby limiting the self-healing effect of the coating. Overall, the mechanism by which a coating possesses self-healing properties is mainly as follows: when the coating is subjected to external forces, such as scratches or impacts, damage may occur either on its surface or internally, resulting in the breakage of dynamic disulfide bonds and hydrogen bonds between molecular chains. Among them, disulfide bonds, by virtue of their dynamic reversibility and exchange reaction properties, are able to recombine under suitable conditions, while hydrogen bonds, owing to their reversibility, can also restore their connections under specific conditions [52,53]. Within a certain temperature range, the polymer molecular chain segments can move more freely. Due to the reversibility of these dynamic disulfide bonds and hydrogen bonds, the broken dynamic reversible bonds can be effectively recombined [54]. This recombination is crucial for the self-healing performance of the coating, ultimately allowing it to restore its original properties (Fig. 7 h). In addition, the fluidity and low viscosity of the polymer chains play a key role in this process. They enable the polymer chains to flow smoothly and fill the cracks, further facilitating the self-healing process of the coating [55]. This self-healing property not only extends the service life of the coating, but also improves its reliability and durability.

Tiny scratches or the early stages of wear inside machinery and equipment, in confined spaces, or in specific environments (such as dark or dim environments) are often difficult to observe directly with the naked eye (Fig. 8a-a2). To realize the early wear monitoring function, the introduction of CQD endowed BNC-SWPU coating with a significant blue fluorescence effect under UV excitation. Compared with the coating scratches under visible light ( Fig. 8a), the scratches were clearly discernible as white traces under UV light (Fig. 8b). Moreover, this coating, thanks to its fluorescent properties, could also be used to monitor the self-healing process. During the self-healing process, the white scratches clearly disappeared, and the coating returned to its original blue fluorescent state (Fig. 8b1-b2). This indicated that the damage condition of the coating could be clearly observed under UV light. Meanwhile, the change in its fluorescence effect could serve as an indicator to monitor the self-healing process and the degree of recovery, providing an efficient and convenient method for detecting tiny scratches and monitoring the self-healing process in real-time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological properties of BNC-SWPU coating and self-healing of wear tracks
ce:section.ce:para: Friction coefficient, wear track width and wear rate were used to evaluate the friction reduction and anti-wear properties of BNON/CQD fillers in SWPU matrix. Fig. 9a demonstrated the average friction coefficient and wear track width of pure SWPU and its composite coatings. Initially, the average friction coefficient of pure SWPU coating was around 0.54, and with the addition of lubricating fillers, the average friction coefficient of coatings decreased first and then increased. This phenomenon was attributed to the lubricating effect of BNON filler. The appropriate amount of lubricating filler could form a layer of lubricating film at the contact interface between the coating and steel ball, which effectively isolated the direct contact of the friction pair and thus reduced the friction coefficient. However, it was worth noting that excessive filler particles could damage the uniformity of the coating, resulting in agglomeration, which further impaired the tribological properties of the coating. When the mass fraction of BNON was 2 wt%, the average friction coefficient of BNC4-SWPU coating reached a minimum value, which was about 28 % lower than that of pure SWPU. The width of wear track was also significantly reduced, as could be seen more visually in Fig. 9d-f. Accordingly, the wear depth, wear area, wear volume and wear rate of different SWPU composites were calculated as shown in Fig. 9b-c. The wear rate of all composite coatings was dramatically reduced, with BNC4-SWPU exhibiting the lowest wear rate, which was 89 % lower than that of SWPU. These results indicated that the tribological performance of composite coatings was superior to that of the pure matrix coating.

The wear surface morphology of coatings was characterized by SEM after the friction test. The wear surface of SWPU coating was heavily abraded, with adhesive wear occurring on the surface. And cracks and deep grooves appeared, indicating that microcracks had been generated and diffused inside the coating during the friction process (Fig. 9d1). This phenomenon corresponded to the wide wear track (1.03 mm) and depth (0.009 mm) of coating. With the incorporation of CQD filler, the wear surface morphology of CQD-SWPU coating was flatter compared to pure SWPU, but there were tiny holes or pores, resulting in only a marginal improvement in the wear resistance of coating (Fig. 9e1). It is well-known that BN nanosheets are excellent lubricating materials, and the functionalized BNON can easily enter the friction gap during friction due to its good dispersion, which plays a good role in reducing friction [56]. BNC4-SWPU wear surface exhibited no obvious cracks, grooves, or holes, which can be attributed in the excellent compatibility and interfacial interaction between the modified filler and SWPU matrix (Fig. 9 f1). When microcracks within the coating propagate to the vicinity of well-dispersed BNON-CQD, these particles in the resin matrix prolong the crack paths, absorb more energy, and hinder the further expansion of microcracks, thereby significantly enhancing the wear resistance of composite coating.

The heat and mechanical force generated due to friction during the contact between the sample and steel ball may lead to material transfer and rearrangement, resulting in the formation of friction film. In order to explore the structure of friction film and deduce its formation and mechanism of action, we performed SEM and FIB-TEM observations on the steel ball after friction. As shown in Fig. 10a, the surface of the steel ball paired with SWPU had many lumps of wear debris embedded in it. These adherent debris might be SWPU material that had been scraped off and adhered to the surface of steel ball during the friction process, and the formation of these adherent debris might negatively affect the performance of friction pair. In addition, the debris might also act as abrasive particles to increase the wear of friction partner. The formation of CQD-SWPU transfer film might be attributed to the incorporation of CQD (Fig. 10b). The transfer film exhibited obvious holes or depressions, suggesting that the material surface had undergone damage or uneven wear due to friction. Despite this, the overall structure of transfer film remained relatively intact. This was because CQD possessed lubricating and anti-wear properties, which reduced friction and wear between the composite and steel ball. More importantly, the overall structure of BNON/CQD-SWPU transfer film was more complete, presenting a flat surface devoid of obvious wear defects (Fig. 10c). This indicated that BNON/CQD-SWPU composites successfully formed a stable transfer film during the friction process. The formation of this transfer film might be attributed to the synergistic effect between BNON and CQD. BN itself was a material renowned for its excellent wear resistance, while the small size and highly dispersed nature of CQD allowed them to penetrate into the friction interface. The combination of these two materials, along with their interaction with the matrix, helped to reduce defects and stress concentration within the material, thereby enhancing its wear resistance. This synergistic effect enabled BNON/CQD-SWPU composite to form a continuous and dense transfer film at the friction interface, effectively shielding the composite surface from further wear and damage. Fig. 10d presented the TEM image and friction mechanism diagram of transfer film formed on the surface of steel ball after sliding against BNC4-SWPU. Fig. 10e and f showed the EDS mapping and line scan results of friction film, respectively. The presence of this transfer film layer could be confirmed by the distribution of elements such as B, N, S and O, thus indicating that the co-transfer of filler and matrix dominated the friction mechanism. In addition, iron oxides were detected in the vicinity of transfer film layer, as confirmed by the lattice spacing shown in the HR-TEM inspection in Fig. 10g-h. The lattice stripe spacings of 0.22 nm and 0.29 nm correspond to the (100) and (110) crystal planes of FeO and Fe2O3, respectively. This analysis also suggested that frictional oxidation of steel occurred during sliding due to the direct contact of friction pair and the generation of frictional heat.

Coatings can be severely damaged during prolonged use, leading to a decline in their protective performance. Therefore, endowing coatings with self-healing properties is of utmost importance in extending their service life. To investigate the effect of the combined action of dynamic disulfide and hydrogen bonding on the self-healing performance of composite coatings, the damage repair process of SWPU and BNC4-SWPU composite coatings was verified following the ball-disk friction and wear test. A moderate heating temperature of 70°C and a higher heating temperature of 100°C were chosen to observe the self-healing process of abrasion-damaged samples. Fig. 11a-b illustrated the self-healing of SWPU wear coatings. When heated at 70°C for 1 h, the wear track was still clearly visible, despite showing sign of self-healing. This indicated that, although the dynamic chemical bonds inside the coating had begun to respond and attempt to repair the damage at lower temperatures, the self-healing ability of coating was limited at shorter durations and lower temperatures. When the heating time was extended to 6 h, the surface of coating returned to a flat and smooth state. This suggested that the dynamic chemical bonds within the coating could be more efficiently reorganized and repaired over time and with the continued application of temperature. In order to shorten the heating time, we placed it on a heating plate equipped with a 100°C setting. It could be observed that only 1 h was needed to significantly exert its repairing effect. The surface of coating was obviously restored to its original smooth state. Compared to the repairing effect under the condition of 70°C, the high temperature significantly accelerated the repairing process. The surface of coating was restored to its original smooth state in a short period of time, further demonstrating the strong self-healing ability conferred by the dynamic disulfide and hydrogen bonds introduced into the resin matrix. Meanwhile, we chose the composite coating sample with the best wear resistance to demonstrate its self-healing ability (Fig. 11c-e). The results showed that after the addition of fillers, the wear track of BNC4-SWPU exhibited a self-healing ability similar to that of pure SWPU, which fully demonstrated that the addition of fillers did not weaken the self-healing effect of the composite coating. Therefore, the wear-resistant composite coatings prepared by us could provide excellent protective performance for coatings by virtue of their excellent self-healing ability after suffering certain damages, thus effectively extending their service life.

In this study, we innovatively designed and synthesized a composite coating based on SWPU polymer, which integrated self-healing, wear resistance and damage monitoring capabilities. Through innovative filler combination strategies and molecular structure design, we utilized modified BN/CQD composite fillers and dynamic reversible disulfide/hydrogen bonding mechanisms to synergistically regulate the comprehensive performance of WPU coatings. Due to the combined effect of aromatic disulfide bonds and intramolecular hydrogen bonds, SWPU composite coatings were endowed with notable self-healing capability. In particular, the composite coatings could achieve rapid self-healing in a short period of time (e.g., only 20 min at 110°C) under thermal stimulation. Meanwhile, based on the interaction between the lubricant interface constructed by BNON/CQD on the wear surface and the resin matrix, the wear resistance of the composite coatings was enhanced. Compared with SWPU coating, the friction coefficient and wear rate of BNC4-SWPU decreased by 28 % and 89 %, respectively. Thanks to the fluorescence effect of CQD, the composite coatings could fulfill the purpose of damage and self-healing monitoring. Ultimately, damaged SWPU-based composite coatings can effectively self-healing in thermal environments, including wear tracks, scratches, and extensive damage, which is significant for extending the service life of coatings and enhancing their protective effects.

I write on behalf of myself and all co-authors to confirm that the results reported in the manuscript are original and neither the entire work, nor any of its parts have been previously published. The authors confirm that the article has not been submitted to peer review, nor has been accepted for publishing in another journal. The authors confirm that the research in their work is original, and that all the data given in the article is real and authentic. If necessary, the article can be recalled, and errors will be carefully corrected.

He Yu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ma Sai: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Zhang Hanzhi: Supervision, Investigation.
